potus.name: Pothos
potus.tip: Great for beginners. Tolerates low light and missed waterings.
potus.description: A climbing indoor plant, very hardy and easy to care for. Perfect for purifying the air.

problems.solution: Let the soil dry out well between waterings.
Move it to a brighter spot.
Mist the leaves occasionally.

careAction.fixed: Water every 7 days in warm season; every 14 days in cold.

potus.placementRecommended: Near a window with bright indirect light.
placementAlternatives: 1-2 meters from a north-facing window
Bathroom with natural light — it enjoys the humidity
High shelf where its trailing vines can hang
potus.placementAvoid: Direct midday sun — it scorches variegated leaves.
potus.whyRationale: It's a tropical understory climber: in its habitat it grows beneath the canopy, so it needs filtered light and consistent humidity. Its shade tolerance comes from adapting to the rainforest floor.

monstera.name: Monstera
monstera.tip: Fenestrated leaves appear with good light. Clean the dust off regularly.
monstera.description: A tropical plant with large perforated leaves. A modern interior design classic.

problems.solution: Move it closer to a window with bright indirect light.
Only water when the top few cm of soil are dry.
Group it with other plants or use a humidifier.

careAction.fixed: Water every 7 days in warm; every 14 in cold. Mist the large leaves.

monstera.placementRecommended: 1-2 meters from a large window with bright indirect light.
placementAlternatives: Bright room with sheer curtains
Near an east-facing window (gentle morning sun)
monstera.placementAvoid: Direct midday sun and cold drafts.
monstera.whyRationale: Native to Central American tropical rainforest, it grows as a hemiepiphyte climbing trees to reach filtered light. Its leaf fenestrations only develop with bright light — that's how it resists winds and shares light with lower leaves.

ficus.name: Ficus
ficus.tip: It doesn't like being moved. Once it finds its spot, leave it there.
ficus.description: An elegant, leafy indoor tree. Adds a sophisticated green touch to any space.

problems.solution: Avoid moving it and protect it from cold drafts.
Wipe the leaves with a damp cloth regularly.

problems.cause: Small pot or lack of nutrients
problems.solution: Repot into a larger container in spring.

careAction.fixed: Water every 10 days in warm; every 15 in cold. Don't move it around.

ficus.placementRecommended: Stable spot with bright indirect light, away from doors.
placementAlternatives: Bright living-room corner with filtered light
Near an east- or west-facing window
ficus.whyRationale: Native to monsoon Southeast Asia, it's adapted to filtered light and stable temperatures. Old leaves don't reacclimate well: it sheds foliage when it detects sudden shifts in light, temperature, or watering.

sansevieria.name: Snake Plant
sansevieria.tip: Nearly indestructible. The most common mistake is overwatering.
sansevieria.description: A hardy plant with upright leaves. Perfect for those who forget to water.

problems.solution: Let it dry out completely between waterings, check the roots.

problems.cause: Very dry air or excessive heat
problems.solution: Keep it away from radiators and direct heat sources.
They grow slowly, especially in low light. Be patient.

careAction.fixed: Water every 14 days in warm; every 21 in cold. Less is more.
careAction.soilCheck: Touch the top 3 cm of soil: if dry, water lightly. Its leaves store water.

sansevieria.placementRecommended: Any indirect-light corner, even low-light spots.
placementAlternatives: Hallway or corner without a nearby window
Office with fluorescent lighting
Bedroom with little natural light
sansevieria.placementAvoid: Constantly moist substrate and pots without drainage.
sansevieria.whyRationale: Native to arid regions of West Africa, its succulent leaves store water and withstand long droughts. Its CAM metabolism (nighttime photosynthesis) lets it tolerate low light and missed waterings.

orquidea.name: Orchid
orquidea.tip: Water by soaking for 10 minutes and let it drain well. Never leave standing water.
orquidea.description: An exotic, elegant flower that blooms multiple times a year with proper care.

problems.solution: Expose it to cooler nights to encourage blooming.
Soak the pot in water for 10 minutes and let it drain.
Water more frequently, check that the roots are healthy.

nutrients.homemade: Rice water and banana peel monthly

careAction.fixed: Soak the pot for 10 minutes every 7 days in warm; every 14 in cold. Let it drain.

orquidea.placementRecommended: Near a window with steady bright indirect light.
placementAlternatives: Bathroom with a window — the humidity helps
Bright kitchen away from the stove
orquidea.placementAvoid: Direct midday sun and standing water in the pot.
orquidea.whyRationale: It's a tropical epiphyte: it grows on trees with aerial roots that capture ambient humidity, not standing water. That's why it needs soaking and full drying — root rot is its main cause of death.

calathea.name: Calathea
calathea.tip: Use filtered or rainwater. Tap water chlorine damages the leaves.
calathea.description: An indoor plant with decorative leaves that move following the daylight.

problems.solution: Use filtered water and increase humidity.
Water and mist it, consider a humidifier.
Move it to filtered indirect light.

careAction.fixed: Water every 4 days in warm; every 7 in cold. Use filtered or settled water.

calathea.placementRecommended: A spot with filtered indirect light and high ambient humidity.
placementAlternatives: Bathroom with a window — it loves the humidity
Near a humidifier in the living room
calathea.placementAvoid: Direct sun and tap water with high chlorine.
calathea.whyRationale: Native to the Amazon understory, its patterned leaves evolved to capture canopy-filtered light. Direct sun damages its photosynthetic system, optimized for low intensity — that's why it moves its leaves with the day-night cycle.

cinta.name: Spider Plant
cinta.tip: Produces babies that hang like a cascade. Great for hanging pots.
cinta.description: A very popular trailing plant that produces little plantlets. Easy to propagate and care for.

problems.cause: Sensitivity to chlorine or fluoride in water
problems.solution: Use water that's been sitting out and trim dry tips.

problems.cause: Too much or too little light
problems.solution: Variegated ones need less light than solid green ones.

problems.cause: Young plant or pot too large
problems.solution: Let it mature and become slightly root-bound.

careAction.fixed: Water every 5 days in warm; every 10 in cold. Use settled water to prevent brown tips.

cinta.placementRecommended: High shelf or hanging spot with bright indirect light.
placementAlternatives: Pot with a stand where its babies can dangle
Near a window with sheer curtains
Kitchen ledge with natural light
cinta.placementAvoid: Intense direct sun — variegated leaves burn.
cinta.whyRationale: Native to South Africa, it evolved in grasslands with filtered light and well-drained soils. Its tuberous roots store water, letting it survive short droughts. It's fluoride-sensitive because its native zone has naturally soft water.

palmera-interior.name: Indoor Palm
palmera-interior.tip: Tolerant of low light. Perfect for corners that need some green.
palmera-interior.description: An indoor-adapted palm that brings a tropical touch to any room.

problems.solution: Keep consistent moisture, mist in summer.
Remove old leaves, it's a normal part of growth.
Palms grow slowly, be patient.

careAction.fixed: Water every 7 days in warm; every 14 in cold. Keep moisture steady without waterlogging.

palmera-interior.placementRecommended: Bright corner with indirect light — even low-light spots work.
placementAlternatives: Wide hallway near a distant window
Entrance hall with filtered light
Office corner with diffused natural light
palmera-interior.placementAvoid: Intense direct sun and dry heater air.
palmera-interior.whyRationale: It's a palm from the Mexican understory: in nature it grows beneath tall trees that filter light. Its fronds adapted to capture dim light, which is why it tolerates shade better than most palms and grows slowly.

aloe-vera.name: Aloe Vera
aloe-vera.tip: The gel in its leaves is great for burns. Needs good drainage.
aloe-vera.description: A popular medicinal succulent. Easy to care for with many uses for skin care.

problems.solution: Let it dry out completely, make sure it drains well.
Needs several hours of sun, ideally direct.
Gradually acclimate it to direct sunlight.

careAction.soilCheck: Touch the soil at 3 cm: if dry, water deeply. Better under than over-water.

aloe-vera.placementRecommended: Near a south- or west-facing window with several hours of direct sun.
placementAlternatives: Sunny balcony during spring and summer
Kitchen counter with direct morning sun
aloe-vera.placementAvoid: Dark spots without sun — it weakens and stretches.
aloe-vera.whyRationale: It's a succulent native to the Arabian Peninsula and North Africa, adapted to arid climates with intense sun. Its fleshy leaves store water and gel for long droughts. Its only real vulnerability is rot from overwatering.

lavanda-angustifolia.name: English Lavender
lavanda-angustifolia.tip: Prune after flowering to keep it compact and prevent it from getting too woody.
lavanda-angustifolia.description: A Mediterranean aromatic with fragrant flowers. Attracts pollinators and repels mosquitoes. The hardiest of the lavenders, tolerating very cold winters.

problems.solution: Prune 1/3 after flowering, never cut into old wood.
Needs very well-draining soil.
Plant in very well-drained soil, avoid overwatering.

nutrients.homemade: Not needed; light compost once a year

careAction.fixed: Water sparingly — every 10 days in warm, every 21 in cold. Soil dry between waterings.

lavanda-angustifolia.placementRecommended: Full sun — at least 6 hours direct, well-drained soil.
placementAlternatives: Mediterranean garden bed facing south
Large pot with sandy substrate and gravel at the bottom
lavanda-angustifolia.placementAvoid: Waterlogged clay soil and damp shade — root rot.
lavanda-angustifolia.whyRationale: Native to the Mediterranean, it evolved in dry calcareous soils with intense sun. It's the most cold-hardy of the three lavenders: it withstands hard frosts down to -15°C thanks to its compact tissues and protective essential oils.

lavanda-stoechas.name: French Lavender
lavanda-stoechas.tip: Choose this if your winter is mild. Doesn't tolerate hard frosts like English lavender, but blooms more in spring and is more decorative with its "rabbit ear" bracts.
lavanda-stoechas.description: French Lavender (Lavandula stoechas) is distinguished by its purple "rabbit ear" bracts above each flower. It's more heat-loving than English lavender and blooms abundantly in spring. Ideal for climates with mild winters.

problems.solution: Cover with frost cloth or move it indoors in pots if hard frosts are expected.
Prune one third after each flowering to rejuvenate.

nutrients.homemade: No fertilizer; a bit of compost once a year

careAction.fixed: Water every 7 days in warm; every 14 in cold. Cover with frost cloth if temperatures drop below -5°C.

lavanda-stoechas.placementRecommended: Full sun and sandy soil — its bracts attract butterflies.
placementAlternatives: Sunny garden bed edge with minimal watering
Pot on a balcony sheltered from cold wind
lavanda-stoechas.placementAvoid: Hard frosts without protection — doesn't tolerate below -5°C.
lavanda-stoechas.whyRationale: Endemic to the western Mediterranean, its "rabbit ear" bracts work as reflectors that attract pollinators from afar. More heat-loving than English lavender, it blooms intensely in spring but doesn't withstand hard frosts.

lavanda-dentada.name: Fringed Lavender
lavanda-dentada.tip: It's the most heat-loving of the lavenders. Blooms almost year-round in warm climates. Recognize it by its serrated or toothed leaf edges.
lavanda-dentada.description: Fringed Lavender (Lavandula dentata) has scalloped leaf edges that distinguish it visually. It's the most heat-tolerant variety and the most cold-sensitive of the three. In warm temperate climates it can bloom practically year-round with regular pruning.

problems.solution: Plant it in the most sheltered spot in the garden or in a pot to move in winter.
Reduce watering; prefers dry conditions between waterings.

nutrients.type: Very low, well-drained and poor soil
nutrients.homemade: No fertilizer; a bit of compost once a year

careAction.fixed: Water every 7 days in warm; every 14 in cold. Plant in a sheltered spot away from cold.

lavanda-dentada.placementRecommended: Full sun in a sheltered area — the most heat-loving and most cold-sensitive.
placementAlternatives: Movable pot to bring indoors in winter
Garden bed against a south-facing wall
lavanda-dentada.placementAvoid: Hard frosts and heavy soils — it's the most delicate of the lavenders.
lavanda-dentada.whyRationale: Native to the warm Mediterranean (southern Spain, North Africa), it evolved in mild climates without harsh frosts. Its toothed, aromatic leaves have more surface area of essential oils — more fragrance, but less cold tolerance.

petunia.name: Petunia
petunia.tip: Remove spent flowers to extend blooming all summer long.
petunia.description: A colorful flower perfect for balconies and planters. Blooms abundantly all summer.

problems.solution: They need full sun and bloom fertilizer every 2 weeks.
Pinch the tips to encourage branching.
Treat with potassium soap or garlic water.

nutrients.homemade: Banana peel tea weekly during blooming

careAction.fixed: Water every 2 days in warm; every 4 in cold. Deadhead spent flowers to extend bloom.

petunia.placementRecommended: Full sun — at least 6 hours direct for abundant flowering.
placementAlternatives: Hanging basket on a sunny balcony
Window planter on a balcony edge with daily watering
Sunny garden bed in borders and corners
petunia.placementAvoid: Dense shade — leggy stems and few flowers.
petunia.whyRationale: An annual native to the Argentine Andes, it evolved in poor volcanic soils under intense high-altitude sun. Continuous blooming is the accelerated reproductive strategy of a short-lived plant before the first frost.

hortensia.name: Hydrangea
hortensia.tip: Flower color depends on soil pH. Acidic = blue, alkaline = pink.
hortensia.description: A shrub with large spherical blooms that change color based on soil acidity.

problems.solution: Only prune old flowers, the buds hold next year's blooms.
They need plenty of water, especially in summer.
Acidify the soil with iron sulfate.

nutrients.homemade: Rusty nails in watering water + banana peel tea

careAction.fixed: Water deeply every 3 days in summer (at the base, no leaf-wetting); every 7 in winter.

hortensia.placementRecommended: Partial sun — morning yes, afternoon shade to avoid scorching the flowers.
placementAlternatives: East-facing bed with rich, acidic soil
Large pot with acid-loving plant substrate
Patio with filtered light and ambient humidity
hortensia.placementAvoid: Midday sun and dry soil — leaves wilt instantly.
hortensia.whyRationale: Native to East Asia (Japan, Korea), it evolved in humid valleys of deciduous forest. The blue-vs-pink color shift by pH is real chemistry: anthocyanins change with aluminum availability, which the plant only absorbs in acidic soil.

jazmin.name: Jasmine
jazmin.tip: The fragrance is stronger at night. Perfect near windows or patios.
jazmin.description: An aromatic climber with small white flowers and an intoxicating scent, especially at dusk.

problems.solution: More sun and low-nitrogen fertilizer.
Drastically reduce watering in cold weather.
Prune after flowering to keep it in check.

careAction.fixed: Water every 4 days in warm; every 10 in cold. Prune hard after flowering.

jazmin.placementRecommended: Full or partial sun — fragrance most intense at dusk.
placementAlternatives: Pergola with morning sun and a sturdy support
West-facing wall covered by the climber
Large pot with trellis on a warm patio
jazmin.placementAvoid: Prolonged hard frost and pots without support — collapses without a trellis.
jazmin.whyRationale: A climber from the Caucasus and western Himalayas, it evolved on forest edges scaling trees toward the light. Its night fragrance attracts crepuscular moths — the scent intensifies at dusk, when those pollinators activate.

geranio.name: Geranium
geranio.tip: A balcony classic. Remove dead flowers and pinch stems for more blooms.
geranio.description: The quintessential balcony plant. Abundant, colorful blooms for months on end.

problems.solution: They need at least 6 hours of direct sun.
Let it dry between waterings, ensure good drainage.
Remove affected parts, treat with Bacillus thuringiensis.

nutrients.homemade: Banana peel tea weekly during blooming season

careAction.fixed: Water every 3 days in warm; every 7 in cold. Remove dead flowers and pinch tips.

geranio.placementRecommended: Full sun — at least 6 hours direct for abundant flowering.
placementAlternatives: South-facing balcony planter
Hanging basket for trailing varieties
Sunny garden bed with well-drained soil
geranio.placementAvoid: Shade and constantly wet soil — stems turn black.
geranio.whyRationale: Native to South Africa (the Cape region), it evolved in a Mediterranean climate with dry summers and mild winters. Its succulent stems store water and let it survive droughts; that's why excess watering is its main cause of death in pots.

albahaca.name: Basil
albahaca.tip: Pinch the tips before it flowers to keep it bushy and productive.
albahaca.description: An essential kitchen herb. Easy to grow in a pot by the kitchen window.

problems.cause: Heat stress or lack of pruning
problems.solution: Cut off flowers as soon as they appear.
Protect it from temperatures below 10°C (50°F).
Fertilize every 2 weeks with organic feed.

careAction.fixed: Water every 2 days in warm; every 4 in cold. Pinch the tips weekly to encourage branching.

albahaca.placementRecommended: Full or partial sun — 4-6 hours, ideally morning light.
placementAlternatives: Pot near tomatoes on a sunny balcony
Herb bed alongside parsley and chives
Indoor counter with direct window light
albahaca.placementAvoid: Intense midday sun without frequent watering — wilts fast.
albahaca.whyRationale: An annual native to tropical Southeast Asia, it needs warmth and long days. Once it flowers, the leaves turn bitter; pinching the tips keeps leaf production going and extends the harvest.

romero.name: Rosemary
romero.tip: Extremely hardy. It prefers being neglected over being pampered.
romero.description: A very hardy Mediterranean herb. Great for cooking and with medicinal properties.

problems.solution: Let it dry well, needs very well-drained soil.
Paradoxically, it prefers poor soil.
Improve air circulation, reduce watering.

nutrients.homemade: Not needed; a bit of compost yearly

careAction.fixed: Water every 10 days in warm; every 21 in cold. Harvest sprigs for cooking without pruning more than a third.

romero.placementRecommended: Full sun — 6+ hours direct, in well-drained soil.
placementAlternatives: Terracotta pot in full sun on the balcony
Mediterranean bed with lavender and thyme
Dry garden border in full sun
romero.placementAvoid: Waterlogged soil or deep shade — root rot sets in fast.
romero.whyRationale: A perennial Mediterranean shrub, evolved on chalky hillsides with dry summers. Its essential oils protect it from heat; overwatering kills it faster than drought. Poor soil intensifies the aroma.

menta.name: Mint
menta.tip: Very invasive. Best kept in an isolated pot or it'll take over the garden.
menta.description: A vigorous herb perfect for teas and cocktails. Grows fast and is very easy to cultivate.

problems.solution: It likes consistent moisture, fertilize in summer.
More light intensifies the essential oils.
Always keep it in a pot, trim runners.

careAction.fixed: Water every 2 days in warm; every 4 in cold. Keep the soil moist and trim invasive runners.

menta.placementRecommended: Partial sun — 4 morning hours, avoiding strong afternoon sun.
placementAlternatives: Isolated pot on the patio (always — keeps it contained)
Hanging planter with generous drainage
Container bed with other moisture-loving herbs
menta.placementAvoid: Planting directly in the garden — its runners take over the space.
menta.whyRationale: A humid-subtropical perennial with very aggressive stoloniferous roots. It evolved along stream banks where constant moisture and lateral spread give it a competitive edge. That's why it always belongs in a pot.

tomatera.name: Tomato Plant
tomatera.tip: Remove suckers (side shoots) for bigger tomatoes.
tomatera.description: The queen of the urban garden. With sun and care, you'll have delicious tomatoes on your balcony.

problems.solution: Water consistently, add crushed eggshells to the soil.
Fertilize regularly, remove old leaves.
Don't wet the leaves, treat with copper fungicide.

careAction.fixed: Water deeply every 2 days in warm; every 3 in cold. Stake the stems and remove side suckers.

tomatera.placementRecommended: Full sun — 8 direct hours in deep, compost-rich soil.
placementAlternatives: 30+ liter pot with a tall stake
Vegetable bed with marigold nearby (pest repellent)
Vertical planter with staking system
tomatera.placementAvoid: Late frost and partial shade — won't fruit without ≥18°C nights.
tomatera.whyRationale: An annual Andean Solanaceae (South America), domesticated some 7,000 years ago. Fruit set depends on sustained warmth and full light; irregular watering causes fruit cracking and blossom end rot from calcium starvation.

pimiento.name: Pepper Plant
pimiento.tip: Wait for them to ripen on the plant for the best flavor. They need warmth.
pimiento.description: A versatile garden fruit. Needs warmth and patience, but the reward is worth it.

problems.solution: Protect from extreme heat (>35°C/95°F) and keep watering regular.
Fertilize with potassium-rich feed when it flowers.
Check the underside of leaves, treat if pests are found.

careAction.fixed: Water every 2 days in warm; every 3 in cold. Sow after last frost and stake if the variety needs it.

pimiento.placementRecommended: Full sun — 8 hours and shelter from cold wind.
placementAlternatives: 25+ liter pot with compost
Bed alongside tomatoes (same conditions)
Warm, bright balcony planter
pimiento.placementAvoid: Shade and late frost — fruits stay green or flowers drop.
pimiento.whyRationale: An annual Solanaceae from the Andes and Central America. Its hot or sweet fruits (capsaicin) evolved as a defense against mammals. It needs ≥18°C nights and pollinators so flowers set instead of dropping.

frutilla.name: Strawberry
frutilla.tip: Place straw under the fruits to keep them off damp soil.
frutilla.description: A sweet fruit you can grow in pots and planters. Perfect for small spaces.

problems.solution: Place mulch or straw under the fruits.
Reduce fertilizer, or use a low-nitrogen one.
Normal in autumn; in summer it may be phosphorus deficiency.

careAction.fixed: Water at the base every 2 days in warm; every 3 in cold. Mulch with straw to keep fruit off the soil.

frutilla.placementRecommended: Full or partial sun — 6 hours, in loose, rich substrate.
placementAlternatives: Hanging pot or sunny planter
Bed with straw mulch between plants
Vertical planter with generous drainage
frutilla.placementAvoid: Fruit touching damp soil — they rot from Botrytis fungus.
frutilla.whyRationale: A perennial Rosaceae of temperate climates, propagating by stolons (runners) instead of seed. Productivity drops after the third year; replace old plants. Wet leaves favor fungus, so watering goes at the base.

limonero.name: Lemon Tree
limonero.tip: Protect it from hard frosts. In a pot, you can bring it indoors in winter.
limonero.description: A citrus tree that adapts well to large pots. Produces fragrant flowers and useful fruit.

problems.solution: Apply iron chelate, acidify the water.
Water regularly, the plant self-regulates its fruit load.
Remove manually, treat with neem oil.

nutrients.homemade: Diluted vinegar in watering + rusty nails monthly

careAction.fixed: Water deeply every 5-7 days in warm weather; less in cold. Fertilize quarterly with a citrus formula rich in iron and zinc.

limonero.placementRecommended: Full sun — at least 6-8 hours. Deep, well-drained soil sheltered from cold wind.
placementAlternatives: 50+ liter pot with drainage on a sunny patio
Bed sheltered against a north-facing wall
Glassed-in porch or greenhouse in frost-prone areas
limonero.placementAvoid: Hard frosts (<-3°C / 27°F) and alkaline soil — drops leaves and develops chronic chlorosis.
limonero.whyRationale: A citrus from the Rutaceae family, native to Southeast Asia. Unlike the orange tree, it fruits almost continuously in subtropical climates. Iron chlorosis on alkaline soils reflects its constant need for micronutrients.

suculenta-generica.name: Succulent
suculenta-generica.tip: Less is more. Well-draining soil and very infrequent watering.
suculenta-generica.description: A plant that stores water in its leaves. Perfect for travelers or forgetful waterers.

problems.solution: Let it dry out completely, check roots for rot.
Gradually move it closer to a sunny window.
Give it a thorough watering and wait, it'll recover.

careAction.soilCheck: Touch the soil: only water when it's completely dry (1-2 weeks in warm weather, 3-4 in cold). Better to underwater.

suculenta-generica.placementRecommended: Full sun or bright direct light — at least 4-6 hours to maintain a compact shape.
placementAlternatives: Shelf near a south-facing window
Outdoor pot with good drainage in spring and summer
Sandy substrate with perlite or pumice for drainage
suculenta-generica.placementAvoid: Waterlogged soil and prolonged shade — rots roots and stretches reaching for light (etiolation).
suculenta-generica.whyRationale: Succulents use CAM metabolism: they close stomata during the day to prevent water loss and open them at night. Their fleshy leaves or stems store water for long droughts — that's why overwatering, not drought, is what kills them most.

cactus.name: Cactus
cactus.tip: Barely water in winter. They need a dry period to bloom.
cactus.description: A desert plant adapted to drought. Very decorative and minimal maintenance.

problems.solution: Stop watering, cut the healthy part and let it callus over.
Permanent scars, avoid sudden sun exposure.
A cool (5-10°C/40-50°F) dry winter triggers blooming.

careAction.soilCheck: Touch the soil: only water if dry to the bottom. In winter, barely water at all — needs dry rest to flower.

cactus.placementRecommended: Full direct sun — at least 6 hours. Sandy substrate with critical drainage.
placementAlternatives: Patio or south-facing balcony with direct sun
Terracotta pot with 50% perlite or coarse sand
Outdoor bed with rocky, well-drained soil
cactus.placementAvoid: Prolonged shade and constantly wet soil — rots the base and stretches deformed.
cactus.whyRationale: Cactaceae family, native to the arid zones of the Americas. Spines are modified leaves and the green stem replaces leaf photosynthesis. Uses CAM metabolism (nocturnal stomata) to tolerate the desert; a cool dry winter triggers blooming.

echeveria.name: Echeveria
echeveria.tip: Avoid wetting the rosette. Water from below or directly onto the soil.
echeveria.description: A rosette-shaped succulent with colors ranging from green to pink. Very decorative.

problems.solution: More sun will intensify colors and compact the shape.
Never wet the leaves, only water the soil.
Gently remove them, it's a natural process.

careAction.soilCheck: Touch the soil: only water when completely dry. Water from below or directly onto the soil, never on the rosette.

echeveria.placementRecommended: Full sun or bright direct light — at least 4-6 hours to keep color and compact form.
placementAlternatives: Shelf near a south-facing window
Sunny balcony in spring and summer
Counter with direct morning light
echeveria.placementAvoid: Prolonged shade — the rosette opens and loses color; standing water in the rosette — rots the center.
echeveria.whyRationale: Crassulaceae from semi-arid mountainous regions of Mexico. Its rosette form minimizes water loss and it uses CAM metabolism. Pastel colors intensify with direct sun; in shade they turn green and the plant stretches (etiolation).

espatifilo.name: Peace Lily
espatifilo.tip: Those white 'flowers' are actually modified leaves called spathes. They bloom more in bright indirect light.
espatifilo.description: A classic office and home plant known for its elegant white blooms and air-purifying abilities. Thrives in low to medium light.

problems.solution: Give it a good soak — it'll perk back up within hours.

problems.cause: Low humidity or chemicals in tap water
problems.solution: Use filtered water and mist the leaves regularly.
Move it to a brighter spot with indirect light.

careAction.fixed: Water every 5 days in warm; every 8 in cold. If leaves droop, water now — it bounces back.

espatifilo.placementRecommended: Spot with medium indirect light; tolerates shadier corners.
placementAlternatives: Bathroom with dim light — it enjoys the humidity
Hallway or bedroom with little natural light
Office away from windows
espatifilo.placementAvoid: Direct sun and tap water with high chlorine.
espatifilo.whyRationale: Native to the tropical understory of Colombia and Venezuela, it evolved beneath dense rainforest canopy. That's why it blooms in shade — rare among houseplants — and signals thirst dramatically: leaves collapse and recover within hours of watering.

dracaena.name: Dracaena
dracaena.tip: Tolerates low light surprisingly well, making it perfect for dim corners.
dracaena.description: A tree-like indoor plant with long, arching leaves. Comes in many varieties and adds a bold vertical accent to any room.

problems.cause: Fluoride or chlorine in tap water
problems.solution: Switch to filtered or distilled water.
Let the top half of the soil dry out before watering again.
Move it to a spot with bright indirect light.

careAction.fixed: Water every 14 days in warm; every 21 in cold. Use filtered water to avoid brown tips.

dracaena.placementRecommended: Corner with medium indirect light; handles low light well.
placementAlternatives: Living-room corner with filtered light
Wide hallway or entrance hall
Room with light filtered by curtains
dracaena.placementAvoid: Prolonged direct sun and tap water with fluoride.
dracaena.whyRationale: Native to East Africa, it evolved on rainforest edges and wooded savannas with filtered light. It's fluoride-sensitive because its native zone has naturally soft water. Brown tips signal mineral-salt buildup that the roots can't process.

filodendro.name: Philodendron
filodendro.tip: Give it something to climb or let it trail — either way, it'll look amazing.
filodendro.description: A trendy tropical plant that comes in climbing and trailing varieties. Easy to care for and fast-growing with gorgeous foliage.

problems.solution: Move it closer to a window with bright indirect light.
Let the top inch of soil dry out between waterings.
Keep it out of harsh direct sun — it prefers filtered light.

careAction.fixed: Water every 7 days in warm; every 14 in cold. Let the top few cm dry between waterings.

filodendro.placementRecommended: Near a window with bright indirect light and a moss pole to climb.
placementAlternatives: High shelf where its stems can trail
Bright corner with a moss pole
Hanging basket near a window
filodendro.placementAvoid: Direct sun and dark corners without natural light.
filodendro.whyRationale: It's a tropical Central American hemiepiphyte: in its habitat it climbs trunks toward the light, growing larger leaves as it rises. That's why a moss pole lets it express its adult form — without it, leaves stay small.

jade.name: Jade Plant
jade.tip: Traditionally thought to bring prosperity and good luck — that's why it's also called the money plant.
jade.description: A thick-leafed succulent with a tree-like growth habit. Also known as the money plant, it's long-lived and easy to care for.

problems.solution: Water only when the soil is completely dry and avoid cold drafts.
Repot in fresh dry soil and cut away any rotted roots.

problems.cause: Salt deposits from water or fertilizer
problems.solution: Wipe leaves clean and flush the soil with distilled water.

careAction.soilCheck: Touch the soil at 3 cm: if completely dry, water deeply. Drought-tolerant like a camel.

jade.placementRecommended: Near a window with bright indirect light or gentle morning sun.
placementAlternatives: Sheltered balcony with filtered sun
Bright shelf near an east-facing window
jade.placementAvoid: Constantly damp soil and deep shade without light.
jade.whyRationale: It's a South African succulent, adapted to semi-arid climates with seasonal droughts. Its leaves and trunk store water — that's why it lives for decades on minimal watering. Its anatomy is designed for scarcity, not abundance: excess water rots it.

peperomia.name: Peperomia
peperomia.tip: Its compact size makes it perfect for small spaces, desks, and shelves.
peperomia.description: A diverse genus of compact plants with fascinating leaf patterns and textures. Great for adding variety to your collection without taking up much room.

problems.solution: Let the soil dry out well — these guys store water in their leaves.
Keep it in a warm spot away from cold windows.
Move to a brighter location with indirect light.

careAction.fixed: Water every 10 days in warm; every 15 in cold. Its leaves store water — don't oversaturate.

peperomia.placementRecommended: Desk or shelf with medium indirect light, away from cold windows.
placementAlternatives: Office desk with diffused natural light
Kitchen ledge with filtered light
Nightstand near an east-facing window
peperomia.placementAvoid: Intense direct sun and cold drafts.
peperomia.whyRationale: It's a tropical American understory plant that grows on fallen logs. Its semi-succulent leaves store water to survive between seasonal canopy rains. That's why it prefers spaced waterings and filtered light — its natural niche.

rosa.name: Rose
rosa.tip: Prune regularly to encourage more blooms — cut just above an outward-facing bud.
rosa.description: The most cultivated ornamental flower in the world. Comes in countless colors and varieties, from miniatures to climbers.

problems.solution: Remove affected leaves, improve air circulation, and treat with fungicide.
Spray with a baking soda solution and ensure good airflow.
Blast them off with water or treat with insecticidal soap.

careAction.fixed: Water deeply twice a week in warm; monthly in winter. Prune in late winter before bud break.

rosa.placementRecommended: Full sun — at least 6 hours direct, deep rich soil.
placementAlternatives: Main garden bed with room to grow
Large pot (min. 40L) on a sunny patio
Border with drip irrigation for deep watering
rosa.placementAvoid: Shade and stagnant air — favor black spot and fungal disease.
rosa.whyRationale: Cultivated by humans for over 5000 years, modern roses come from Asian and European hybrids. They bloom on new wood: each winter cut stimulates flower-bearing buds. Deciduous, they use winter dormancy to build up reserves.

bougainvillea.name: Bougainvillea
bougainvillea.tip: It actually needs a bit of drought stress to bloom its best — don't baby it with water.
bougainvillea.description: A spectacular climbing plant covered in vibrant colorful bracts. Loves heat and full sun, and rewards neglect with stunning displays.

problems.symptom: No flowers, lots of green leaves
problems.solution: Cut back on watering and use a low-nitrogen fertilizer.
Keep it in a stable warm spot — it hates being moved.
Needs at least 6 hours of direct sun to bloom properly.

careAction.fixed: Water sparingly — every 7 days in warm, every 21 in cold. Water stress induces flowering.

bougainvillea.placementRecommended: Intense full sun — at least 8 hours direct, against a warm wall.
placementAlternatives: Pergola or south-facing wall for maximum heat
Large pot (min. 50L) on a sunny terrace
bougainvillea.placementAvoid: Frost and excess water — drops bracts and stays plain green.
bougainvillea.whyRationale: Native to tropical South America (Brazil, Peru), it evolved in seasonally dry forest. Its colorful bracts aren't flowers but modified leaves protecting the tiny flowers within — they attract birds and butterflies while confusing herbivores.

hibisco.name: Hibiscus
hibisco.tip: Each flower only lasts about a day, but healthy plants keep producing new ones nonstop.
hibisco.description: A tropical shrub famous for its large, showy flowers in vivid colors. A real showstopper in any warm garden or sunny patio.

problems.solution: Keep watering regular and protect from sudden cold snaps.
Check drainage and feed with a balanced fertilizer.
Treat with neem oil or insecticidal soap.

nutrients.homemade: Banana peel tea + coffee grounds monthly

careAction.fixed: Water every 3 days in warm; every 7 in cold. Keep humidity steady without waterlogging.

hibisco.placementRecommended: Full or partial sun — frost protection in cold climates.
placementAlternatives: Large movable pot to bring indoors in winter
Sunny bed against a warm wall
Patio sheltered from cold wind
hibisco.placementAvoid: Frost (below 10°C) and sudden relocation — drops buds.
hibisco.whyRationale: Native to tropical Asia (China, India), it evolved in warm climates without true winter. Each flower lasts a day: the strategy is to produce large showy flowers in continuous succession to attract hummingbirds and tropical bees.

margarita.name: Daisy
margarita.tip: Deadhead spent flowers regularly to keep new blooms coming all season.
margarita.description: A classic, cheerful garden flower that's easy to grow and brightens up any space. Great for borders, pots, and cutting gardens.

problems.solution: Remove faded flowers to redirect energy into new blooms.
Move to a sunnier spot — daisies love full sun.

problems.cause: Poor air circulation and damp conditions
problems.solution: Space plants out and avoid overhead watering.

careAction.fixed: Water every 3 days in warm; every 7 in cold. Deadhead spent flowers and divide the clump every 2-3 years.

margarita.placementRecommended: Full sun — at least 6 hours direct for abundant flowering.
placementAlternatives: Sunny bed at the garden border
Large pot for a sunny balcony
Mixed border alongside other perennials
margarita.placementAvoid: Dense shade and waterlogged soil — weak stems and powdery mildew.
margarita.whyRationale: A perennial native to central and southern Europe, it evolved in temperate montane grasslands. Dividing the clump every 2-3 years mimics the natural rejuvenation by low-intensity fires that cleared its native habitat.

perejil.name: Parsley
perejil.tip: Always harvest the outer stems first — this lets the center keep producing new growth.
perejil.description: An essential kitchen herb packed with flavor and nutrients. Easy to grow in pots on a windowsill or in the garden.

problems.solution: Make sure the pot drains well and let the top soil dry slightly.
Provide some afternoon shade in hot weather and harvest frequently.
Pick them off by hand or plant extra to share with them.

careAction.fixed: Water every 2 days in warm; every 4 in cold. Harvest outer stems and let the center keep growing.

perejil.placementRecommended: Partial sun — 4 morning hours; partial shade in summer.
placementAlternatives: Pot near a kitchen window
Cool-herb bed with cilantro
Morning-sun balcony planter
perejil.placementAvoid: Full summer sun without watering — bolts to flower fast.
perejil.whyRationale: A biennial native to the eastern Mediterranean, it prefers cool weather. It flowers in its second year (hence 'biennial'); home gardeners resow each season. Seeds germinate slowly because their oils delay sprouting.

oregano.name: Oregano
oregano.tip: It actually dries really well — hang bunches upside down and store in jars for year-round flavor.
oregano.description: A Mediterranean herb that's a must-have for any kitchen garden. Hardy, drought-tolerant, and packed with aromatic oils.

problems.solution: Keep it on the dry side — stress actually boosts its essential oils.
Improve drainage and water only when the soil is dry.
Give it full sun and trim regularly to keep it bushy.

nutrients.homemade: Not needed; light compost once a year

careAction.fixed: Water every 7 days in warm; every 14 in cold. Prune hard in spring to renew the clump.

oregano.placementRecommended: Full sun — 6+ hours; tolerates poor, rocky soil.
placementAlternatives: Mediterranean bed border with rosemary
Full-sun pot with fast drainage
Warm, sunny wall planter
oregano.placementAvoid: Deep shade and waterlogged soil — weak flavor and fungus.
oregano.whyRationale: A Mediterranean perennial adapted to rocky hillsides with dry summers. Water stress concentrates the essential oils — sparse watering yields more flavor than heavy feeding. It tolerates frost and extreme heat with almost no care.

cilantro.name: Cilantro
cilantro.tip: It bolts fast in the heat — sow every few weeks for a continuous harvest.
cilantro.description: A dual-use herb where the leaves are cilantro and the seeds are coriander. A kitchen essential for countless cuisines around the world.

problems.solution: Grow in partial shade during summer and harvest leaves young.
Ensure good drainage — cilantro hates sitting in water.
Gently crush the seeds before sowing to speed things up.

careAction.fixed: Water every 2 days in warm; every 4 in cold. Sow in succession every 3 weeks for continuous harvest.

cilantro.placementRecommended: Partial sun — 4 morning hours; partial shade in summer.
placementAlternatives: Pot on a balcony with morning light
Bed alongside parsley and arugula
Indoor tray with bright diffused light
cilantro.placementAvoid: Strong summer sun — bolts fast under long-day light.
cilantro.whyRationale: An annual native to the Mediterranean and Asia Minor, sensitive to photoperiod: long days and heat trigger bolting and the leaves turn bitter. So sowing in autumn or early spring, harvesting young, works best.

tomillo.name: Thyme
tomillo.tip: Don't worry if the base gets woody — that's totally normal and actually a sign of a mature plant.
tomillo.description: An aromatic Mediterranean herb with tiny leaves and a big flavor. Great in the kitchen and beautiful as ground cover in the garden.

problems.solution: Divide the plant or take cuttings to start fresh.
Plant in gritty, well-draining soil and water sparingly.
Thyme prefers lean soil — skip the fertilizer.

careAction.fixed: Water every 10 days in warm; every 21 in cold. Prune by a third in spring to rejuvenate the clump.

tomillo.placementRecommended: Full sun — 6+ hours, in stony, very-well-drained soil.
placementAlternatives: Herb bed with rosemary and oregano
Terracotta pot in full sun
Dry bed border as ground cover
tomillo.placementAvoid: Heavy clay and damp soil — root rot is almost certain.
tomillo.whyRationale: A Mediterranean perennial subshrub with tiny leaves. Its essential oils (thymol) concentrate under drought and full sun; ample watering dilutes the aroma. It tolerates frost but dies from excess moisture.

lechuga.name: Lettuce
lechuga.tip: Pick the outer leaves and let the center keep growing — you'll harvest for weeks.
lechuga.description: The easiest vegetable to grow at home. Fast-growing, perfect for containers, and you can start eating it in just a few weeks.

problems.cause: Bolting due to heat or stress
problems.solution: Grow in cooler weather or provide afternoon shade.
Set up beer traps or sprinkle diatomaceous earth around the base.
Provide shade cloth and water in the morning.

careAction.fixed: Water every 2 days in warm; every 3 in cold. Pick outer leaves; the center keeps producing.

lechuga.placementRecommended: Partial sun — 4 morning hours; partial shade in summer.
placementAlternatives: Vegetable bed with humus-rich soil
Balcony planter with morning light
Wide pot with worm-casting compost
lechuga.placementAvoid: Full summer sun — bolts to flower and turns bitter.
lechuga.whyRationale: An annual Asteraceae of cool climates; long days and heat send it bolting and the sap turns bitter. That's why succession sowing and early picking work best. Outer-leaf harvesting extends productivity for several weeks.

pepino.name: Cucumber
pepino.tip: Pick them small for the best flavor and crunch — don't let them get too big on the vine.
pepino.description: A productive summer vine that rewards you with fresh, crunchy fruits. Great for salads, pickling, or snacking straight from the garden.

problems.solution: Water deeply and regularly, especially during hot spells.
Space plants well, prune for airflow, and treat with neem oil.
Hand-pollinate if needed and keep moisture consistent.

nutrients.homemade: Banana peel tea weekly + rice water

careAction.fixed: Water deeply every 2 days in warm; every 3 in cold. Stake the vine and attract pollinators with nearby flowers.

pepino.placementRecommended: Full sun — 6+ hours with a stake or trellis to climb.
placementAlternatives: Vegetable bed with vertical mesh
30+ liter pot with a 1.5 m stake
Pergola or trellis in full sun
pepino.placementAvoid: Partial shade and isolation from pollinators — few fruits.
pepino.whyRationale: An annual Cucurbitaceae of Indian origin, with separate male and female flowers on the same plant. It needs insect pollination to set fruit; without bees or hand-pollination, flowers drop fruitless. Inconsistent watering yields bitter cucumbers.

zanahoria.name: Carrot
zanahoria.tip: Loose, rock-free soil is the key to straight, beautiful carrots.
zanahoria.description: A root vegetable that takes patience but delivers satisfying results. Nothing beats pulling a homegrown carrot out of the soil.

problems.solution: Work the soil deeply and remove stones before planting.
Avoid high-nitrogen fertilizer — it sends energy to the leaves.
Cover with fine mesh netting and companion plant with onions.

careAction.fixed: Water evenly every 3 days in warm; every 4 in cold. Thin seedlings to 5 cm when they reach 4 cm.

zanahoria.placementRecommended: Full sun — 6 hours, in deep, loose, stone-free soil.
placementAlternatives: Vegetable bed with sifted, deep soil
Pot at least 30 cm deep
Deep balcony tray with loose substrate
zanahoria.placementAvoid: Compacted or rocky soil — forked or deformed roots.
zanahoria.whyRationale: An Apiaceae biennial: it forms a root in year one and flowers in year two (gardeners harvest before flowering). Eurasian origin; it needs deep, loose soil so the root grows straight. Dense sowing yields small roots.

rucula.name: Arugula
rucula.tip: It grows crazy fast — you can start harvesting baby leaves in just 3 weeks. Great for beginners.
rucula.description: A peppery salad green that's super easy to grow and adds a gourmet touch to any dish. Quick from seed to plate.

problems.solution: Harvest young leaves and grow in cooler weather or partial shade.
Cover with row cover fabric or use neem oil spray.
Sow successively every 2 weeks and grow in light shade.

careAction.fixed: Water every 2 days in warm; every 3 in cold. Sow in succession every 2-3 weeks for continuous harvest.

rucula.placementRecommended: Partial sun — 4 morning hours; partial shade in summer.
placementAlternatives: Bed alongside lettuce (same conditions)
Balcony pot with morning light
Mid-size planter at least 20 cm deep
rucula.placementAvoid: Full summer sun — leaves get too spicy and bolt fast.
rucula.whyRationale: A fast-cycling Brassicaceae annual (4-6 weeks). Its glucosinolates give the characteristic peppery flavor, which intensifies under heat stress. Long days plus warmth trigger early flowering, so successive sowing helps.

naranjo.name: Orange Tree
naranjo.tip: Protect it from frost — even a light freeze can damage flowers and young fruit.
naranjo.description: A beautiful citrus tree with incredibly fragrant blossoms and delicious fruit. Can be grown in large pots in cooler climates.

problems.solution: Apply iron chelate and make sure the soil isn't too alkaline.
Water consistently — some drop is normal as the tree self-regulates.

problems.cause: Honeydew from scale insects or aphids
problems.solution: Treat the pest first with neem oil, then wipe the mold off.

careAction.fixed: Water deeply every 5-7 days in warm weather; less in cold. Feed with a citrus formula in spring and summer for sweet fruit.

naranjo.placementRecommended: Full sun — at least 6-8 hours. Deep, well-drained soil sheltered from cold wind.
placementAlternatives: 50+ liter pot with drainage on a sunny patio
Bed next to a north-facing wall
Glassed-in porch in frost-prone areas
naranjo.placementAvoid: Hard frosts (<-3°C / 27°F) and alkaline soil — drops leaves and flowers fall to chlorosis.
naranjo.whyRationale: A citrus from the Rutaceae family, native to subtropical China. Harvest is seasonal (autumn-winter) and fruit sweetness requires sustained heat. Iron chlorosis on alkaline soil is the most common failure — needs regular micronutrients.

aguacate.name: Avocado
aguacate.tip: Growing one from a pit is a fun project, but don't expect fruit for 5-10 years — enjoy it as a houseplant.
aguacate.description: A tropical tree that's become wildly popular as a houseplant. Its large glossy leaves make a bold statement even without fruit.

problems.cause: Low humidity or salt buildup in soil
problems.solution: Mist regularly and flush the soil with clean water periodically.
Let the soil dry out between waterings and check root health.
Move to a bright spot — avocados need lots of light to thrive.

nutrients.homemade: Worm castings + banana peel tea monthly

careAction.fixed: Water deeply every 5-7 days in warm weather; less in cold. Drainage is critical — won't tolerate waterlogging. Feed compost in spring.

aguacate.placementRecommended: Full sun — at least 6-8 hours. Deep, well-drained soil, protected from wind.
placementAlternatives: Open bed with room for extensive roots
Very large pot (80+ liters) with generous drainage
Warm patio sheltered from frost
aguacate.placementAvoid: Waterlogged soil and frost below 10°C (50°F) — root rot is the most common cause of death.
aguacate.whyRationale: Lauraceae, native to Mesoamerica. Takes 3-7 years to fruit and needs cross-pollination (type A and B varieties). Roots are shallow — lack of oxygen from waterlogging kills more plants than drought.

higuera.name: Fig Tree
higuera.tip: Once it's established, it's incredibly hardy — figs are tough survivors.
higuera.description: An ancient Mediterranean fruit tree prized for its sweet fruit and beautiful lobed leaves. Surprisingly adaptable to pots and smaller gardens.

problems.solution: Keep watering consistent, especially when fruit is forming.
Remove affected leaves and improve air circulation.
Be patient — figs can take 2-3 years to fruit. Make sure you have a self-pollinating variety.

nutrients.homemade: Banana peel tea + wood ash biweekly

careAction.fixed: Water deeply every 7-10 days in warm weather; nearly stop in winter (it's deciduous). Formative pruning in winter while bare.

higuera.placementRecommended: Full sun — at least 8 hours. Well-drained soil; tolerates poor and rocky soils.
placementAlternatives: Open bed with room for a generous canopy
Very large pot (60+ liters) limiting growth
Next to a north-facing wall that holds heat
higuera.placementAvoid: Strong wind and pruning during sap flow — the white latex irritates skin and wounds heal slowly.
higuera.whyRationale: Moraceae, deciduous Mediterranean tree. Yields two crops in a good year: brebas in spring (on old wood) and figs in summer (on new shoots). White latex is the family hallmark; tolerates poor soils and dry summers.

mandarino.name: Mandarin Tree
mandarino.tip: It's one of the best citrus trees for growing in pots — compact and generous with fruit.
mandarino.description: A compact citrus tree that produces sweet, easy-to-peel fruit. Perfect for container growing on a sunny patio or balcony.

problems.solution: Remove affected leaves and apply neem oil as prevention.
Feed with a citrus-specific fertilizer in spring and summer.
Water consistently — sudden soaking after dry spells causes splitting.

careAction.fixed: Water deeply every 5-7 days in warm weather; less in cold. Fertilize quarterly with a citrus formula rich in iron and zinc.

mandarino.placementRecommended: Full sun — at least 6-8 hours. Good drainage; ideal for small gardens or large pots.
placementAlternatives: 40+ liter pot with drainage on a sunny patio
Bed next to a north-facing wall
Glassed-in porch in frost-prone areas
mandarino.placementAvoid: Hard frosts (<-3°C / 27°F) and alkaline soil — drops leaves and develops chronic chlorosis.
mandarino.whyRationale: Rutaceae, native to Southeast Asia. More compact than the orange tree, with sweet fruit and thin peel. Harvest is seasonal, generally earlier than the orange. Shares the family-wide sensitivity to iron chlorosis on alkaline soil.

zapallito.name: Summer Squash
zapallito.tip: Harvest them young and small — they taste way better and the plant keeps producing more.
zapallito.description: A super productive summer vegetable that practically grows itself. One plant can feed a whole family with tender, versatile squash.

problems.solution: Improve airflow, water at the base, and treat with a baking soda spray.

problems.cause: Blossom end rot or sitting on wet soil
problems.solution: Place a board under fruit and keep watering even.
Hand-pollinate by transferring pollen from male to female flowers with a brush.

careAction.fixed: Water deeply every 2 days in warm; every 3 in cold. Pick young and hand-pollinate if flowers drop.

zapallito.placementRecommended: Full sun — 6+ hours, with generous compost and ample space.
placementAlternatives: Vegetable bed with 1-1.5 m between plants
40+ liter pot with compost
Wide tray in full sun near pollinators
zapallito.placementAvoid: Cramped space and isolation from pollinators — few fruits.
zapallito.whyRationale: An annual Cucurbitaceae of South American origin (Cucurbita maxima domesticated in the Andes). Separate male and female flowers need pollinators; when bees are absent, female flowers drop without setting and hand-pollination helps.

ciboulette.name: Chives
ciboulette.tip: Cut them right at the base and they'll regrow quickly — like a haircut for your herbs.
ciboulette.description: A mild onion-flavored herb that's dead simple to grow. Pretty purple flowers are edible too. Great in pots or garden borders.

problems.solution: Divide clumps every 2-3 years to keep them vigorous.
Let the soil dry slightly between waterings.
Cut all foliage to the ground — it'll regrow clean.

careAction.fixed: Water every 3 days in warm; every 6 in cold. Cut stems to 3 cm above the soil and they regrow vigorously.

ciboulette.placementRecommended: Partial sun — 4-6 hours, in cool, well-drained soil.
placementAlternatives: Pot near a kitchen window
Herb bed with parsley and cilantro
Vegetable bed border as a carrot companion
ciboulette.placementAvoid: Full summer sun without watering — yellow tips and weak stems.
ciboulette.whyRationale: A perennial Allium of cool temperate zones in the northern hemisphere. Its vigorous regrowth after cutting (similar to grass) makes it inexhaustible; the underground bulb survives hard frost and resprouts in spring.

haworthia.name: Haworthia
haworthia.tip: Unlike most succulents, it actually tolerates more shade — perfect for spots other succulents can't handle.
haworthia.description: A mini succulent with gorgeous striped or windowed leaves. Slow-growing, low-maintenance, and adorable on any desk or shelf.

problems.solution: Water sparingly — only when the soil is completely dry.
Move to bright indirect light — it doesn't need direct sun.
Provide some shade and keep it in a cooler spot.

careAction.soilCheck: Touch the soil: only water when completely dry (2-3 weeks in warm, 4+ in cold). Strong sun burns its leaves.

haworthia.placementRecommended: Bright indirect light — not direct. Tolerates shade better than almost any other succulent.
placementAlternatives: Desk near an east- or north-facing window
Shelf with diffuse natural light
Bathroom with indirect light — tolerates ambient humidity
haworthia.placementAvoid: Intense direct sun and constantly wet soil — the first burns the leaves, the second rots the base.
haworthia.whyRationale: Asphodelaceae, native to semi-arid South Africa with partial shade. Its translucent tips (windows) allow photosynthesis inside the leaf — a rare adaptation that makes it shade-tolerant. Still uses CAM and stores water.

sedum.name: Burro's Tail
sedum.tip: Handle with extreme care — the plump little leaves fall off at the slightest touch.
sedum.description: A trailing succulent with cascading stems covered in bead-like leaves. Stunning in hanging pots, but very delicate to handle.

problems.solution: Place it somewhere it won't be bumped and water sparingly.
Give it a good drink — the leaves should plump back up.
Move to a brighter spot with several hours of indirect sun.

careAction.soilCheck: Touch the soil: only water when completely dry. Handle as little as possible — leaves drop at the slightest brush.

sedum.placementRecommended: Full sun or bright direct light — at least 4-6 hours. Hanging pot or high shelf where stems can trail.
placementAlternatives: Hanging pot on a bright porch
High shelf near a north-facing window
Balcony with direct sun in spring and summer
sedum.placementAvoid: Walkways where it gets brushed — drops leaves; prolonged shade and wet soil — rots the stems.
sedum.whyRationale: Crassulaceae from Mexico (burro's tail), more cold-tolerant than echeveria. Its bead-shaped leaves drop at the slightest touch but each one roots easily — an evolutionary clonal-propagation strategy on rocky soil. Uses CAM like the family.

yuca.name: Yucca
yuca.tip: Yucca is an all-rounder: it handles drought, direct sun, and even some cold. Don't overwater it.
yuca.description: The Yucca elephantipes or indoor Yucca is a woody-trunked plant with long, stiff, pointed leaves. Extremely hardy and tolerant of varied light conditions, though it prefers direct sun. Ideal for those seeking a large, low-maintenance plant.

problems.solution: It's normal to lose old leaves. If there are many, reduce watering and let it dry well between waterings.
Stop watering immediately. If severely affected, cut the healthy part of the trunk and replant.

problems.cause: Very dry air or too much sun
problems.solution: Trim the dry tips and if it's in very strong direct sun, move it to bright indirect light.

careAction.fixed: Water every 10 days in warm; every 21 in cold. Forgives missed waterings easily.

yuca.placementRecommended: Near a large window with several hours of direct sun.
placementAlternatives: Sunny balcony or covered patio in summer
Bright corner with very strong indirect light
yuca.placementAvoid: Dark corners — it stretches looking for light and loses its form.
yuca.whyRationale: Native to semi-arid zones of Mexico and Central America, it evolved to tolerate intense sun, droughts, and poor soils. Its woody trunk stores reserves to withstand extreme conditions. Its only real weak point is rot from overwatering.

jacaranda.name: Jacaranda
jacaranda.tip: Water sparingly once established; tolerates summer drought and blooms in November without much watering.
jacaranda.description: The Jacaranda is the most emblematic tree of Buenos Aires, painting the city violet in November. A large tree, ideal for spacious gardens or sidewalks. Once established, it tolerates summer drought and needs little care.

problems.solution: Needs several years to bloom; reduce watering and let it stress a bit in summer.
Improve drainage and apply iron chelate if soil is alkaline.

nutrients.homemade: Mature compost once a year in spring

careAction.fixed: Water every 14 days in warm; every 30 in cold. Once established, it barely needs watering.

jacaranda.placementRecommended: Full sun and ample space — large tree, extensive root system.
placementAlternatives: Wide sidewalk or large garden with deep soil
Main garden bed away from pipes
jacaranda.placementAvoid: Near foundations or pipes — invasive roots when mature.
jacaranda.whyRationale: Endemic to northwestern Argentina and southern Bolivia, it evolved in warm valleys with seasonal droughts. Deciduous in winter, it blooms in spring BEFORE leafing out — maximizing the violet flowers' visibility for pollinators.

ceibo.name: Ceibo (Cockspur Coral Tree)
ceibo.tip: Prune after flowering so it doesn't get woody. Blooms in summer, especially near water.
ceibo.description: The Ceibo is the Argentine national flower, a tree or shrub that grows naturally on river and lagoon banks. Its scarlet red flowers are spectacular in summer. Very hardy and easy to grow once established.

problems.solution: Needs full sun; young specimens take 2-3 years to bloom.
Improve drainage; ceibo tolerates moisture but not permanent waterlogging.

careAction.fixed: Water every 10 days in warm; every 21 in cold. Prune after flowering to maintain shape.

ceibo.placementRecommended: Full sun and moist soil — tolerates being near water.
placementAlternatives: Pond edge or low garden with humidity
Sunny bed with regular watering
ceibo.placementAvoid: Dense shade and prolonged dry soil — few flowers and weak stems.
ceibo.whyRationale: Argentina's national flower, native to the riverbanks of the NEA and southern Brazil. Its scarlet bloom attracts migrating hummingbirds; its roots have nitrogen-fixing nodules that let it grow in poor riverside soils.

glicina.name: Wisteria
glicina.tip: Prune hard in winter and again in summer (green pruning) so it focuses energy on flowering. Without pruning, it grows uncontrollably.
glicina.description: Wisteria is a spectacular climber with cascades of fragrant lilac flowers in spring. It can live for decades and grow huge. It needs a very strong support structure and regular pruning to bloom well.

problems.cause: Excess nitrogen or lack of pruning
problems.solution: Avoid nitrogen fertilizers; prune in winter and summer with green pruning.
Prune twice a year without exception to keep it contained.

nutrients.homemade: Wood ash + banana tea in spring

careAction.fixed: Water every 7 days in warm; every 21 in cold. Prune hard in winter and summer to force flowering.

glicina.placementRecommended: Full sun and a very strong support — iron pergola or solid wall.
placementAlternatives: Sturdy pergola over a wide patio
Brick wall with metal anchors
glicina.placementAvoid: Fragile structures and proximity to pipes — enormous weight and invasive roots.
glicina.whyRationale: A deciduous climber native to China and Japan, it evolved in temperate mountain valleys. Its fragrant violet flowers are bee-pollinated; the winter photoperiod (cold + short days) is required to induce flower buds.

gardenia.name: Gardenia
gardenia.tip: Use chlorine-free water and never wet the flowers. It's demanding, but the perfume is worth the effort.
gardenia.description: The Gardenia is famous for its intensely fragrant white flowers. It's a demanding plant that needs soil acidity, chlorine-free water, high humidity, and protection from intense cold. When it blooms, it's one of the most beautiful in the garden.

problems.cause: Sudden temperature change, drafts, or irregular watering
problems.solution: Keep it in a stable spot with no drafts; water consistently without waterlogging.

problems.cause: Chlorosis from alkaline soil or iron deficiency
problems.solution: Acidify the substrate and apply iron chelate.
Never wet the flowers; mist only the foliage.

nutrients.homemade: Coffee grounds weekly + acidified water with vinegar (1 tbsp/L)

careAction.fixed: Water every 3 days in warm with chlorine-free water; every 7 in cold. Never wet the flowers.

gardenia.placementRecommended: Partial sun — morning yes, afternoon shade, acid and moist soil.
placementAlternatives: Bed alongside other acid-loving plants (camellia, azalea)
Pot with acid-loving substrate and rainwater
gardenia.placementAvoid: Strong midday sun and chlorinated tap water — chlorosis and bud drop.
gardenia.whyRationale: Native to southern Asia, it evolved in tropical understory with acidic, humid soils. It only absorbs iron and magnesium at pH 5-6: that's why it yellows (chlorosis) in the alkaline soils typical of Buenos Aires without acidification.

camelia.name: Camellia
camelia.tip: Plant it where afternoon sun won't reach; flowers burn in midday heat. Blooms in winter, when most of the garden sleeps!
camelia.description: The Camellia is a winter-flowering shrub with perfect petals in pink, white, and red. It prefers semi-shade, acid soils, and protection from strong sun. In Buenos Aires it blooms from autumn to spring, filling the garden with color in the coldest months.

problems.cause: Direct midday sun or rain on flowers
problems.solution: Plant with east orientation; morning sun is ideal.
Acidify with aluminum sulfate or azalea substrate.

problems.cause: Very alkaline soil or excess nitrogen
problems.solution: Adjust pH to 5.5-6.5 and use low-nitrogen fertilizer.

nutrients.homemade: Coffee grounds monthly + rainwater when possible

careAction.fixed: Water every 7 days in warm; every 14 in cold. Keep substrate acidic and ambient humidity high.

camelia.placementRecommended: East-facing partial shade — gentle morning sun, afternoon shade.
placementAlternatives: Under a deciduous tree that gives summer shade
Bed against an east-facing wall
camelia.placementAvoid: Midday sun — burns flowers and leaves; alkaline soil — chronic chlorosis.
camelia.whyRationale: Native to East Asia, it evolved in deciduous montane understory. It blooms in winter-spring because the buds need exposure to low temperatures (vernalization) to open properly.

dalia.name: Dahlia
dalia.tip: In autumn, when leaves turn black from frost, dig up the tubers, dry them, and store in a cool dry place until spring.
dalia.description: Dahlias are the queens of the summer garden with huge flowers in every color and form. They are tubers planted in spring that bloom from summer to autumn. In cold climates the tubers must be lifted to overwinter. They bring spectacular color to the garden.

problems.solution: Treat with potassium soap or neem oil; ladybugs are natural allies.

problems.cause: Powdery mildew from humidity and poor ventilation
problems.solution: Improve air circulation and treat with diluted baking soda.

problems.cause: Lack of nutrients or overcrowded plant
problems.solution: Fertilize high-phosphorus and remove some shoots to concentrate energy.

careAction.fixed: Water every 3 days in warm; every 7 in cold. In autumn, dig up tubers and store dry until spring.

dalia.placementRecommended: Full sun — at least 6 hours direct, deep rich soil.
placementAlternatives: Sunny bed with regular watering
Large pot (min. 30L) with stake for tall varieties
dalia.placementAvoid: Late frost on planted tubers — rots them before sprouting.
dalia.whyRationale: Native to Mexico and Central America, the tubers evolved as reserves to survive the dry season. In cold winters you have to lift them in autumn: hard frosts shatter the cells and rot them before sprouting.

salvia-ornamental.name: Ornamental Sage
salvia-ornamental.tip: Remove old flowers so it keeps producing new ones. Very drought-resistant once established; don't over-water.
salvia-ornamental.description: Ornamental Sage attracts hummingbirds and butterflies with its flower spikes in blue, violet, or red. It's a perennial or annual depending on the variety, very heat- and drought-resistant. Ideal for pollinator gardens and bed borders.

problems.solution: Improve drainage; sage prefers to dry between waterings.

problems.cause: Lack of pruning or low light
problems.solution: Remove old flowers regularly and ensure full sun.

careAction.fixed: Water every 7 days in warm; every 14 in cold. Remove old flowers to extend blooming.

salvia-ornamental.placementRecommended: Full sun — attracts hummingbirds and butterflies, ideal for pollinator gardens.
placementAlternatives: Bed border with other Mediterranean plants
Sunny pot on patio or balcony
Pollinator garden alongside lavender and rosemary
salvia-ornamental.placementAvoid: Frequent watering and waterlogged soil — rots quickly.
salvia-ornamental.whyRationale: Native to Brazil and warm zones of South America, it evolved in savannas and dry forest. Its red or violet spikes attract hummingbirds; the aromatic leaves contain essential oils that repel herbivores — typical sage chemistry.

cala.name: Calla Lily
cala.tip: Plant in semi-shade and keep soil moist; in hot summer it needs more water. Blooms better with some winter cold.
cala.description: The Calla or Arum Lily is an elegant plant with funnel-shaped, pure white flowers. It grows from a rhizome and prefers moist soils, even tolerating being near water. Blooms in spring and early summer. In Argentine gardens it's a classic of shade and semi-shade.

problems.cause: Excess direct sunlight or lack of watering
problems.solution: Move to semi-shade and keep substrate moist.
In autumn, reduce watering so the plant rests before winter.
Improve drainage and avoid water pooling in leaf axils.

nutrients.homemade: Rice water + worm compost every 15 days during blooming

careAction.fixed: Water every 5 days in warm; every 10 in cold. In autumn reduce watering to induce dormancy.

cala.placementRecommended: Semi-shade — filtered light, consistently moist soil, tolerates proximity to water.
placementAlternatives: Bed under a deciduous tree
Pond edge or moist garden zone
Patio pot with abundant indirect light
cala.placementAvoid: Intense direct sun and dry soil — scorched leaves and dormant rhizome.
cala.whyRationale: Endemic to South Africa (Cape), it evolved in seasonal wetlands with humid summers and cool winters. The rhizome needs a dry-cold rest period to build up reserves; without that autumn pause, the next bloom will be weak.

copete.name: Marigold
copete.tip: Plant near tomatoes and peppers — repels nematodes and aphids. Remove dry flowers so it keeps blooming all season.
copete.description: The Marigold or Tagetes is a summer annual flower with a double role: it decorates with yellow and orange flowers and protects the vegetable garden from pests like nematodes and aphids. Easy to grow, heat-resistant, fast-growing. Ideal for bed borders and pots.

problems.cause: Spider mites in heat and dry conditions
problems.solution: Water more frequently and treat with potassium soap.

problems.cause: Lack of sun or flowers not removed
problems.solution: Ensure 6+ hours of sun and remove faded flowers regularly.

careAction.fixed: Water every 3 days in warm; every 5 in cold. Remove faded flowers for continuous blooming.

copete.placementRecommended: Full sun — at least 6 hours direct, ideal next to the vegetable garden.
placementAlternatives: Vegetable bed border to repel nematodes
Pot alongside tomatoes and peppers
Sunny balcony planter
copete.placementAvoid: Shade and waterlogged soil — few flowers and prone to fungus.
copete.whyRationale: Native to Mexico and Central America, its roots secrete thiophenes that repel soil nematodes — a natural fungicide. A classic companion for the vegetable garden: protects tomatoes and peppers while attracting pollinators.

verbena.name: Verbena
verbena.tip: Let it self-seed; verbena bonariensis comes back on its own every year. Cut dry flowers to prolong blooming.
verbena.description: Verbena bonariensis is a native Argentine plant that attracts butterflies and bees with small violet flowers on thin tall stems. Very drought-tolerant and almost no care needed once established. It self-seeds, making it a practical perennial.

problems.solution: Improve air circulation and avoid wetting leaves when watering.
Verbena blooms best in poor soils; avoid excess nitrogen fertilizer.

careAction.fixed: Water every 7 days in warm; every 14 in cold. Cut dry flowers to prolong blooming.

verbena.placementRecommended: Full sun — attracts butterflies and bees, ideal for pollinator gardens.
placementAlternatives: Bed border alongside other native plants
Sunny pot on patio or balcony
Wild garden where it can self-seed
verbena.placementAvoid: Very fertile soil and excess fertilizer — more leaves, fewer flowers.
verbena.whyRationale: An Argentine native, it evolved in pampas and forest edges with poor soils and dry summers. Its tall slender stems with violet flowers attract pollinators seeking height above the vegetation; self-seeding makes it a practical perennial.

romero-rastrero.name: Trailing Rosemary
romero-rastrero.tip: Ideal for balconies and walls; grows hanging. Water very little and give it all the sun you can. Same care as upright rosemary but more decorative in height.
romero-rastrero.description: Trailing Rosemary is a prostrate variety of rosemary that, instead of growing upward, spreads horizontally or cascades down. Perfect for hanging pots, terraces, or wall coverings. As hardy and aromatic as common rosemary, with a more decorative silhouette.

problems.solution: Cut dry parts, improve drainage, and space waterings.

problems.cause: Soil too fertile or excessive shade
problems.solution: Poor soil and full sun intensify essential oils.

nutrients.homemade: No fertilizer; light compost once a year

careAction.fixed: Water every 10 days in warm; every 21 in cold. Trim sprigs to guide the cascading habit.

romero-rastrero.placementRecommended: Full sun — 6+ hours, ideal in a hanging pot or tall wall.
placementAlternatives: Hanging pot on a sunny balcony
Ground cover in a dry sunny bed
Wall edge or vertical planter
romero-rastrero.placementAvoid: Waterlogged soil or deep shade — root rot like upright rosemary.
romero-rastrero.whyRationale: A prostrate variety of common rosemary, same Mediterranean origin and same drought-adapted physiology. The trailing form doesn't change the needs: full sun, poor soil, sparse watering; ideal for ground cover or decorative cascades.

tomate-cherry.name: Cherry Tomato
tomate-cherry.tip: Cherry tomatoes are indeterminate — they keep growing all season. Give them a tall stake and remove side shoots so they concentrate energy on the fruits.
tomate-cherry.description: Cherry Tomato produces small sweet tomatoes in abundant clusters all summer. It's indeterminate, meaning it keeps growing and producing until frost. Ideal for large pots (minimum 30L), balconies, and small vegetable gardens. Needs less space than the large tomato but the same care.

problems.solution: Keep watering consistent; sudden moisture changes cause splitting.
Consistent watering + crushed eggshells in substrate.
Treat with potassium soap or neem oil; introduce lacewings if possible.

nutrients.homemade: Banana tea weekly when fruits start

careAction.fixed: Water deeply every 2 days in warm; every 3 in cold. Pick almost daily and stake tall.

tomate-cherry.placementRecommended: Full sun — 8 hours, in a 30+ L pot with a tall stake.
placementAlternatives: 30+ liter pot with a 1.5 m stake
Small vegetable bed with vertical mesh
Sunny balcony against a warm wall
tomate-cherry.placementAvoid: Late frost and irregular watering — fruits split when water hits dry soil.
tomate-cherry.whyRationale: A Solanum lycopersicum variant with small fruit and an indeterminate growth habit (it keeps going until frost). Same genetics as the large tomato, but you harvest almost daily; abundant clusters need a tall stake and sucker pruning to focus energy on the fruit.
